terms.definition: A fraction representing one part out of ten equal parts; equivalent to the decimal 0.1.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Each strip on the fraction wall shows 1/10 of the whole.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 1/10 is larger than 1/5 because 10 is bigger than 5.

terms.definition: A way of telling the time that divides the day into two 12-hour periods (a.m. and p.m.).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: On a 12-hour clock, 3 o'clock in the afternoon is written as 3:00 p.m.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a.m. and p.m. — a.m. is before noon, p.m. is after.

terms.definition: A flat shape with only two dimensions: length and width; no thickness or depth.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A circle, triangle, and rectangle are all 2-D shapes.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 2-D and 3-D — 2-D shapes are flat and cannot be picked up.

terms.definition: A way of telling the time using numbers from 00:00 to 23:59, without needing a.m. or p.m.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: On a 24-hour clock, 3 p.m. is written as 15:00.
terms.common_errors: Adding 12 to times before noon — only add 12 to p.m. times.

terms.definition: A solid shape with three dimensions: length, width, and height (or depth).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cube and a sphere are 3-D shapes — they take up space.
terms.common_errors: Calling a 2-D drawing of a cube a 3-D shape.

terms.definition: An abbreviation meaning 'before noon' (from Latin 'ante meridiem'), used with 12-hour clock times.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: School starts at 8:50 a.m.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a.m. (morning) with p.m. (afternoon/evening).

terms.definition: Approximately; a word used when giving a rough estimate rather than an exact answer.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The pencil is about 15 centimetres long.

terms.definition: Describing an angle that measures less than 90 degrees.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This angle is acute because it is smaller than a right angle.
terms.common_errors: Confusing acute (less than 90°) with obtuse (more than 90°).

terms.definition: An angle measuring less than 90 degrees — sharper and smaller than a right angle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 45° angle is an acute angle.
terms.common_errors: Calling a right angle (90°) acute.

terms.definition: To combine fractions with the same denominator by adding their numerators and keeping the denominator the same.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Add fractions: 2/5 + 1/5 = 3/5.
terms.common_errors: Adding both numerators and denominators: writing 2/5 + 1/5 = 3/10 instead of 3/5.

terms.definition: To line up digits in the correct place-value columns when setting out a written calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: From French 'aligner' (to arrange in a line)
terms.example_usage: Align the ones digits in the same column before adding.
terms.common_errors: Not lining up digits correctly, causing place-value errors.

terms.definition: A clock with a circular face and moving hands that point to the hours and minutes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read the time on this analogue clock.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the hour hand (short) with the minute hand (long).

terms.definition: A connecting word used in number names between hundreds and tens (e.g. one hundred and twenty-three).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 305 in words: three hundred and five.
terms.common_errors: Omitting 'and' in spoken number names — UK convention includes it.

terms.definition: The amount of turn between two lines that meet at a common point, measured in degrees.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Identify the angles in this shape — are they acute, right, or obtuse?
terms.common_errors: Thinking the angle depends on the length of the lines — it doesn't.

terms.definition: Close to but not exact; a value estimated rather than precisely calculated.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give an approximate answer to 298 + 403.

terms.definition: About; used to indicate a value that is close to but not exactly a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The playground is approximately 50 metres long.

terms.definition: A rectangular diagram used to represent multiplication, with dimensions showing the factors and internal sections showing partial products.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use an area model to show 23 × 4 as (20 × 4) + (3 × 4).
terms.common_errors: Not partitioning correctly into tens and ones.

terms.definition: Equal in length when comparing two measurements or objects.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This ribbon is as long as that one — they are both 30 cm.

terms.definition: A reference line on a graph or chart used for plotting data; the horizontal is the x-axis, vertical is the y-axis.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the axis along the bottom with the days of the week.
terms.common_errors: Not labelling axes on a graph.

terms.definition: A graph that uses rectangular bars of different heights to compare quantities across categories.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a bar chart to show the number of children choosing each sport.
terms.common_errors: Drawing bars of different widths — only the height should vary.

terms.definition: A familiar reference value used for estimating or comparing, such as 10, 100, or 1/2.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use 1/2 as a benchmark — is this fraction more or less than a half?

terms.definition: An older term for exchanging in subtraction; now more accurately called 'exchange' or 'regroup'.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Some people say 'borrow' but it is more accurate to say 'exchange' one ten for ten ones.
terms.common_errors: Using 'borrow' implies the value needs returning — 'exchange' is the preferred term.

terms.definition: The outer edge or perimeter of a shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure the boundary of the rectangle to find its perimeter.

terms.definition: A mental strategy for crossing a tens boundary when adding or subtracting, e.g. 47 + 5 = 47 + 3 + 2.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Bridge through 10: to add 7 + 5, first add 3 to make 10, then add 2 more.

terms.definition: To work out the answer to a mathematical problem using an operation or method.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate 345 + 267 using column addition.

terms.definition: Transferring a value from one place-value column to the next when the sum in a column exceeds 9.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: When carrying in column addition, write the carried digit below the next column.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to add the carried digit.

terms.definition: To select the most suitable method, operation, or approach for a calculation or problem.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Choose the best method to solve this: mental, written, or drawing?

terms.definition: To sort shapes or numbers into groups based on their properties.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Classify these shapes: which are quadrilaterals?

terms.definition: To build a number from its component parts, such as combining hundreds, tens, and ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Compose 400 + 30 + 7 to make 437.

terms.definition: To recognise links between mathematical ideas, facts, or representations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Connect the multiplication 3 × 4 = 12 to the division 12 ÷ 3 = 4.

terms.definition: A mathematical relationship or link between facts, ideas, or operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the connection between 3 × 5 = 15 and 30 × 5 = 150?

terms.definition: To build or draw a shape accurately using appropriate tools such as a ruler and set square.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Construct a rectangle with sides of 5 cm and 3 cm.
terms.common_errors: Not using tools accurately, resulting in imprecise shapes.

terms.definition: To change from one unit of measurement to another while keeping the same quantity.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Convert 2 metres to centimetres: 2 × 100 = 200 cm.
terms.common_errors: Multiplying when you should divide, or vice versa.

terms.definition: Reciting the multiples of 8 in order: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.common_errors: Losing the pattern — the 8 times table is harder to memorise.

terms.definition: Reciting the multiples of 50 in order: 50, 100, 150, 200 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: Reciting the multiples of 4 in order: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: Starting from a number and counting in decreasing steps, often used as a subtraction strategy.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use counting back to solve 43 - 5: 43, 42, 41, 40, 39, 38.
terms.common_errors: Including the starting number in the count, leading to an answer one too high.

terms.definition: A number that uses a decimal point to show tenths, hundredths, or other fractional parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.5 is a decimal that means five tenths or one half.
terms.common_errors: Thinking longer decimals are always bigger — 0.5 is greater than 0.42.

terms.definition: The dot in a decimal number that separates the whole-number part from the fractional part.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 3.7, the decimal point separates the 3 (ones) from the 7 (tenths).
terms.common_errors: Ignoring the decimal point when comparing numbers.

terms.definition: To break a number down into its place-value parts or other useful components.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Decompose 356 into 300 + 50 + 6.

terms.definition: An informal introduction to measuring turn size; children experience angles as turns before using a protractor.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This angle is greater than a right angle — in degrees it would be more than 90.

terms.definition: A type of clock that shows the time using digits only (e.g. 14:30), not hands on a face.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The digital clock shows 09:45.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that 09:45 digital is the same as quarter to ten analogue.

terms.definition: How far apart two points or places are, measured in standard units.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure the distance between the two posts.

terms.definition: A rule stating that multiplying a sum by a number gives the same result as multiplying each addend separately and then adding.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Using the distributive law: 4 × 13 = (4 × 10) + (4 × 3) = 40 + 12 = 52.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to multiply both parts when using the distributive law.

terms.definition: To split a number into 10 equal groups; each digit moves one place to the right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Divide 30 by ten: 30 ÷ 10 = 3.
terms.common_errors: Removing a zero instead of understanding the place-value shift.

terms.definition: A known division result related to a times table fact.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If 4 × 3 = 12, then the division fact is 12 ÷ 4 = 3.

terms.definition: To create a shape, line, or diagram accurately using appropriate tools.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a line exactly 8 cm long using a ruler.
terms.common_errors: Not starting from zero on the ruler.

terms.definition: The length of time that something lasts, measured in hours, minutes, and seconds.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The duration of the film is 1 hour and 45 minutes.
terms.common_errors: Calculating duration across the hour boundary incorrectly.

terms.definition: The multiplication facts for the number 8: 1×8=8, 2×8=16, through to 12×8=96.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Practise the eight times table: 8, 16, 24, 32...
terms.common_errors: Confusing the 8 times table with the 3 times table at higher multiples.

terms.definition: The final point, position, or value in a sequence, measurement, or calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure from one end of the line to the other.

terms.definition: To count or list items one by one in an organised way.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Enumerate all the possible combinations of colours.

terms.definition: The real-world setting in which mathematical concepts are applied or observed.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Look for right angles in our environment — where can you see them?

terms.definition: A whole number that can be divided exactly by 2 with no remainder; ending in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 14 is an even number because 14 ÷ 2 = 7 with no remainder.
terms.common_errors: Including 0 or excluding 0 — 0 is an even number.

terms.definition: To predict what a likely or reasonable answer should be before calculating.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I expect the answer to 298 + 305 will be about 600.

terms.definition: The number 50; five groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Fifty pence is half of one pound.
terms.common_errors: Confusing fifty (50) with fifteen (15).

terms.definition: Able to use different methods or approaches depending on the numbers in a calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Be flexible — sometimes mental methods are better than written methods.

terms.definition: The multiplication facts for the number 4: 1×4=4, 2×4=8, through to 12×4=48.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The four times table is double the two times table.

terms.definition: The horizontal line between the numerator and denominator of a fraction, representing division.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the fraction 3/4, the fraction bar means 3 divided by 4.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that the fraction bar means division.

terms.definition: Finding a fraction of a quantity by dividing by the denominator and multiplying by the numerator.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find 1/3 of 12: divide 12 by 3 to get 4.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that 'of' means multiply in this context.

terms.definition: A complete rotation of 360 degrees, ending back at the starting position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A full turn brings you back to where you started.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a full turn (360°) with a half turn (180°).

terms.definition: A method for multiplication that uses a grid to organise partial products by place value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the grid method to multiply 34 × 6: split 34 into 30 and 4.
terms.common_errors: Not adding the partial products together at the end.

terms.definition: Exactly in the middle between two values or points.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What number is halfway between 30 and 40? It is 35.

terms.definition: Going straight across from left to right, parallel to the horizon.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a horizontal line across the page.
terms.common_errors: Confusing horizontal (across) with vertical (up and down).

terms.definition: Units of time; each hour is 60 minutes long.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The journey takes 2 hours.

terms.definition: A question asking about the duration of time or the length of an object.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How long is the swimming lesson? It is 45 minutes.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 'how long' (duration) with 'what time' (specific time).

terms.definition: The number 100; ten groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One hundred pennies makes one pound.

terms.definition: The number that is 100 smaller than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A hundred less than 450 is 350.
terms.common_errors: Subtracting from the wrong digit — changing the tens instead of the hundreds.

terms.definition: The number that is 100 greater than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A hundred more than 350 is 450.

terms.definition: The place-value column representing groups of one hundred; the third digit from the right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 742, the 7 is in the hundreds column and represents 700.

terms.definition: The specific digit in the hundreds column of a number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 854, the hundreds digit is 8.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the digit (8) with its value (800).

terms.definition: A short dash (-) used between words in compound number names such as twenty-one or thirty-five.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write fifty-three with a hyphen between fifty and three.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting the hyphen in written number names.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 1.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I in Roman numerals means 1.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 2.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: II in Roman numerals means 2.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 3.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: III in Roman numerals means 3.

terms.definition: To read and make sense of information presented in graphs, charts, tables, or diagrams.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Interpret the bar chart — which sport is the most popular?
terms.common_errors: Not reading the scale carefully.

terms.definition: The regular gap between values on a number line or scale, or between marked points on a measuring instrument.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: On this number line, the interval between marks is 5.
terms.common_errors: Assuming intervals are always 1 without checking the scale.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing 4, using the subtractive principle (5 minus 1).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: IV means 4 in Roman numerals — it is one less than V.
terms.common_errors: Writing IIII instead of IV.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing 9, using the subtractive principle (10 minus 1).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: IX means 9 in Roman numerals — it is one less than X.
terms.common_errors: Writing VIIII instead of IX.

terms.definition: A metric unit of length equal to 1,000 metres, abbreviated as km; used for measuring longer distances.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The distance from home to school is about 2 kilometres.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a kilometre is longer than a mile — a kilometre is shorter.

terms.definition: Words or symbols added to a graph, diagram, or shape to identify parts and make it easier to read.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the axes on your bar chart.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to label graphs and diagrams.

terms.definition: A straight one-dimensional mark extending in both directions; in measurement, a specific length between two points.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a straight line from point A to point B.

terms.definition: To pair up equivalent values, shapes, or expressions that represent the same thing.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Match each fraction to its equivalent: 1/2 matches 2/4.

terms.definition: Calculations performed in your head without writing down working, using known facts and strategies.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use mental arithmetic to work out 45 + 28.

terms.definition: Exactly 12 o'clock at night; the start of a new day in both 12-hour and 24-hour time.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Midnight is 12:00 a.m. or 00:00 on a 24-hour clock.
terms.common_errors: Confusing midnight (12 a.m.) with noon (12 p.m.).

terms.definition: A metric unit of length equal to one tenth of a centimetre or one thousandth of a metre, abbreviated as mm.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 10 millimetres in one centimetre.
terms.common_errors: Confusing millimetres with centimetres on a ruler.

terms.definition: Units of time; there are 60 minutes in one hour.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The lesson lasts 30 minutes.

terms.definition: Measurements expressed using two different units together, such as 1 m 30 cm or 2 kg 500 g.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 1 metre 50 centimetres in mixed units: 1 m 50 cm.
terms.common_errors: Not converting correctly between mixed units and single units.

terms.definition: A number that appears in the 100 times table: 100, 200, 300, 400 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Round 287 to the nearest multiple of 100: 300.

terms.definition: Numbers in the 3 times table: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: List the multiples of three up to 30: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30.

terms.definition: A known product from the times tables that can be recalled from memory.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Using the multiplication fact 3 × 8 = 24, what is 30 × 8?

terms.definition: The closest whole minute when reading or rounding a time.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read the time to the nearest minute — the minute hand is between 7 and 8, so it is about 37 minutes past.
terms.common_errors: Not reading the minute hand precisely enough.

terms.definition: Almost; close to but not exactly a value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 298 is nearly 300.

terms.definition: Exactly 12 o'clock in the middle of the day; also called midday.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Noon is 12:00 p.m. or 12:00 on a 24-hour clock.
terms.common_errors: Confusing noon (12 p.m.) with midnight (12 a.m.).

terms.definition: A system of symbols used to write numbers, operations, or mathematical ideas.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Roman numeral notation uses letters like I, V, and X to represent numbers.

terms.definition: Describing an angle that measures more than 90 degrees but less than 180 degrees.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This angle is obtuse because it is wider than a right angle.
terms.common_errors: Confusing obtuse (more than 90°) with acute (less than 90°).

terms.definition: An angle measuring more than 90° but less than 180° — wider than a right angle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 120° angle is an obtuse angle.

terms.definition: One of eight equal parts of a whole, written as 1/8.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If you cut a pizza into 8 equal slices, each slice is one eighth.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 1/8 is bigger than 1/4 because 8 is bigger than 4.

terms.definition: One of five equal parts of a whole, written as 1/5.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One fifth of 20 is 4.

terms.definition: One of ten equal parts of a whole, written as 1/10; equivalent to the decimal 0.1.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One tenth of a metre is 10 centimetres.

terms.definition: One of three equal parts of a whole, written as 1/3.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One third of 15 is 5.
terms.common_errors: Making unequal thirds when dividing.

terms.definition: A number from 0 to 9, consisting of a single digit.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is a one-digit number.

terms.definition: A problem requiring only a single calculation to find the answer.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This is a one-step problem: 15 + 23 = ?

terms.definition: An abbreviation meaning 'after noon' (from Latin 'post meridiem'), used with 12-hour clock times.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: School finishes at 3:30 p.m.
terms.common_errors: Confusing p.m. (afternoon) with a.m. (morning).

terms.definition: A set of two objects or numbers grouped together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find a pair of numbers that add to 10.

terms.definition: Two lines that are always the same distance apart and never meet, no matter how far they are extended.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rectangle has two pairs of parallel sides.
terms.common_errors: Thinking lines that look close together are parallel — they must be equidistant everywhere.

terms.definition: An intermediate result in a multiplication, found by multiplying part of one number by part of another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 23 × 4, the partial products are 20 × 4 = 80 and 3 × 4 = 12.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to add the partial products together.

terms.definition: The pieces that make up a whole; in fractions, the equal sections of a divided whole.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This shape is divided into 4 equal parts.
terms.common_errors: Not making parts equal when working with fractions.

terms.definition: The total distance around the outside edge of a 2-D shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the perimeter of this rectangle by adding all four sides.
terms.common_errors: Confusing perimeter (distance around) with area (space inside).

terms.definition: Two lines that meet at exactly 90 degrees (a right angle).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The sides of a rectangle are perpendicular to each other.
terms.common_errors: Confusing perpendicular (at right angles) with parallel (never meeting).

terms.definition: Something that could happen; a potential outcome in a probability or combinatorics context.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: List all the possibilities when choosing a sandwich and a drink.
terms.common_errors: Missing some possibilities when listing combinations.

terms.definition: To display or show data, calculations, or results in a clear, organised way.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Present your data in a bar chart.

terms.definition: Making sense in the context of the problem; an answer that seems about right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Is 5,000 a reasonable answer for 49 × 10? No — 490 is more reasonable.

terms.definition: To rearrange a number's place-value parts to make a calculation easier, e.g. exchanging 1 ten for 10 ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Regroup 42 as 30 + 12 to make the subtraction easier.
terms.common_errors: Regrouping incorrectly — the total value must stay the same.

terms.definition: A connection between numbers, operations, or mathematical ideas.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the relationship between multiplication and division? They are inverse operations.

terms.definition: Stands for or shows; used when a symbol, picture, or expression stands for a value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Each picture on the pictogram represents 5 people.

terms.definition: A number system from ancient Rome using letters (I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100) to represent values.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 14 in Roman numerals: XIV.
terms.common_errors: Using IIII for 4 instead of IV, or writing VV for 10 instead of X.

terms.definition: Approximately; not exactly but close enough for an estimate.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 302 + 198 is roughly 500.

terms.definition: Fractions that have the same bottom number, making them easy to add, subtract, or compare.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 2/5 and 3/5 have the same denominator, so we can add them: 5/5 = 1.
terms.common_errors: Trying to add fractions with different denominators without finding a common one.

terms.definition: Having equal worth or representing the same amount, even if written differently.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 1/2 and 2/4 have the same value — they are equivalent fractions.

terms.definition: The numbered markings on a measuring instrument or the axis of a graph, showing regular intervals.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read the scale on the ruler — each small mark is 1 millimetre.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the scale goes up in ones when it might go up in twos, fives, or tens.

terms.definition: Multiplying or dividing a value by a constant factor; in Y3, used to describe 'times as many' problems.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Scaling: if Anna has 3 sweets and Ben has 4 times as many, Ben has 12.
terms.common_errors: Confusing scaling (multiplying) with adding — '4 times as many' means multiply, not add 4.

terms.definition: Units of time; there are 60 seconds in one minute.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: From Latin 'secunda' (the second division of the hour)
terms.example_usage: Time how long it takes to run across the playground — count the seconds.

terms.definition: A triangular drawing tool with one or two right angles, used for drawing perpendicular and parallel lines.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a set-square to check that this angle is exactly 90 degrees.

terms.definition: To reduce a fraction to its simplest form by dividing both numerator and denominator by their common factor.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Simplify 4/8: divide both by 4 to get 1/2.
terms.common_errors: Not dividing by the highest common factor, leaving a partly simplified fraction.

terms.definition: Counting forward or backward by a number other than 1, such as counting in 2s, 5s, or 10s.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Skip count in 4s: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20.
terms.common_errors: Losing the pattern at higher numbers.

terms.definition: The exact requirements or constraints for a mathematical task, such as length, angle, or number of sides.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a triangle to this specification: two sides of 5 cm and one right angle.

terms.definition: To write number names correctly in words.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Spell the number 48: forty-eight.
terms.common_errors: Common misspellings: 'fourty' (should be forty), 'eigth' (should be eighth).

terms.definition: The conventional or agreed-upon way of doing something, such as standard units or standard methods.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure in standard units: centimetres, not hand-widths.

terms.definition: The beginning point of a measurement, sequence, or calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Start counting from 0 on the number line.
terms.common_errors: Not starting from zero when measuring with a ruler.

terms.definition: A plan or method chosen to solve a mathematical problem efficiently.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What strategy will you use to add 99? Add 100 and subtract 1.

terms.definition: To take one fraction from another when they have the same denominator, subtracting the numerators.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Subtract fractions: 5/7 - 2/7 = 3/7.
terms.common_errors: Subtracting both numerators and denominators.

terms.definition: Following an orderly, logical approach to ensure nothing is missed.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Be systematic when listing all the possible combinations.

terms.definition: The number that is 10 smaller than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Ten less than 73 is 63.

terms.definition: The number that is 10 greater than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Ten more than 73 is 83.

terms.definition: The digit in the tens column of a number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 374, the tens digit is 7.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the digit (7) with its value (70).

terms.definition: One of ten equal parts; the first decimal place represents tenths.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One tenth of 50 is 5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing tenths (1/10) with tens (10).

terms.definition: The number 1,000; ten groups of one hundred.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are one thousand metres in a kilometre.

terms.definition: The multiplication facts for the number 3: 1×3=3, 2×3=6, through to 12×3=36.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Practise the three times table: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15...

terms.definition: A number from 100 to 999, composed of hundreds, tens, and ones digits.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 456 is a three-digit number.
terms.common_errors: Including 099 or less as three-digit numbers.

terms.definition: A multiplicative comparison phrase meaning one quantity is a given number of groups of another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Sam has 3 times as many stickers as Jo — if Jo has 4, Sam has 12.
terms.common_errors: Adding instead of multiplying — '3 times as many as 4' is 12, not 7.

terms.definition: A multiplicative comparison for continuous quantities such as length, weight, or money.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This rope is 3 times as much as 2 metres, so it is 6 metres.
terms.common_errors: Confusing additive ('3 more than') with multiplicative ('3 times as much').

terms.definition: A multiplicative comparison of heights.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The bookshelf is 3 times as tall as the chair.

terms.definition: A problem requiring two separate calculations to find the answer.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This is a two-step problem: first add, then subtract.
terms.common_errors: Only completing the first step.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 5.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: V in Roman numerals means 5.

terms.definition: To check that an answer is correct by using a different method or the inverse operation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Verify your subtraction by adding: if 85 - 37 = 48, then 48 + 37 should equal 85.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 6.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: VI means 6 in Roman numerals.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 7.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: VII means 7 in Roman numerals.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 8.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: VIII means 8 in Roman numerals.

terms.definition: A number written using words instead of digits.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 345 in word form: three hundred and forty-five.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting hyphens in compound numbers (e.g. forty-five, not forty five).

terms.definition: A calculation set out on paper using a structured layout, such as column addition or grid method.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a written method to calculate 234 + 178.
terms.common_errors: Not aligning digits in the correct columns.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 10.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: X in Roman numerals means 10.
terms.common_errors: Confusing Roman numeral X with the multiplication sign ×.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 11.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: XI means 11 in Roman numerals.

terms.definition: The Roman numeral representing the number 12.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: XII means 12 in Roman numerals — look for it on clock faces.
